sections.heading: Follow-Up
sections.content:
The NCCN Panel recommends close clinical follow-up for patients with MCC starting immediately after diagnosis and treatment. Physical examination should include a complete skin and complete lymph node (LN) examination every 3-6 months for the first 3 years, then every 6-12 months thereafter. The recommended frequency of follow-up visits is purposely broad to allow for an individualized schedule based on the risk of recurrence, stage of disease, and other factors such as patient anxiety and clinician preference. The Panel's recommendation for frequent clinical exams during the first 3 years reflects that MCC will recur in up to half of patients, and most recurrences occur within the first few years after diagnosis [17,18,48,138]. Education regarding self-examination of the skin is useful for patients with MCC because of their increased risk for other nonmelanoma skin cancers (NMSCs) [MCC-6].

Surveillance is critical because recurrence and development of lymph node and distant metastases in MCC are not uncommon [14-19,84,226,320]. Based on data from large retrospective analyses (N > 100), the median time to recurrence in patients with MCC is about 8 to 9 months, with 90% of recurrences occurring within 24 months [17,18,48,138]. Time to local recurrence is generally shorter than for regional recurrence, and time to distant metastasis is longer [16,17,19,225]. Due to the fast-growing nature of the disease, detection of multiple distant metastases at once is not uncommon [125]. Multiple retrospective analyses have shown that recurrence of MCC is associated with poor prognosis [71,73,93,239,252,312], and locoregional recurrence is associated with the development of distant metastasis.

[Second primary malignancies: Patients with MCC are at higher risk for developing second cancers including SCC, BCC, melanoma, CLL, NHLs, lung, breast, and kidney cancers [19,23,35,91,94,97-104], Late effects of radiation therapy including fibrosis, lymphedema (particularly with groin nodal basin irradiation), and secondary malignancies in radiation fields, Late effects of immunotherapy including endocrinopathies and other immune-related adverse events, Chronic immunosuppression-related complications in patients with ongoing immunosuppressive conditions, Lymphedema surveillance in patients who underwent lymph node dissection or regional radiation, Dermatologic surveillance for NMSC given increased risk of second skin cancers]

Based on data from large retrospective analyses, the median time to recurrence is about 8 to 9 months, with 90% of recurrences occurring within 24 months [17,18,48,138]. Locoregional recurrence is associated with the development of distant metastasis, and all types of recurrences (local, regional, and distant) may be associated with poorer DSS and OS [71,73,93,239,252,312]. Detection of multiple distant metastases at once is not uncommon [125]. Patients who have had MCC are at increased risk for a prior, concurrent, or subsequent second primary malignancy [23,35,97-100]. Risk factors for recurrence include immunosuppression, advancing age, advancing stage of disease (stage II-IV), non-SLN metastases, Merkel Cell polyomavirus negative status, as well as additional factors as determined by the treating physicians [20,McEvoy AM, et al. J Am Acad Dermatol 2024;90:569-576], [MCC-6]

timeline.title: First 3 years after diagnosis
timeline.body: Complete skin and lymph node examination every 3-6 months. Patient education on self-examination of skin. Frequent clinical exams due to high recurrence risk (up to 50% of patients, most recurrences within first few years).

timeline.title: After 3 years
timeline.body: Complete skin and lymph node examination every 6-12 months. Continued surveillance for recurrence and second primary malignancies (including NMSC, SCC, BCC, melanoma, CLL, NHLs, lung, breast, kidney).

late_effects.name: Second primary malignancies
late_effects.detail: Patients with MCC are at higher risk for developing second cancers including SCC, BCC, melanoma, CLL, NHLs, lung, breast, and kidney cancers [19,23,35,91,94,97-104]

late_effects.name: Late effects of radiation therapy
late_effects.detail: Including fibrosis, lymphedema (particularly with groin nodal basin irradiation), and secondary malignancies in radiation fields

late_effects.name: Late effects of immunotherapy
late_effects.detail: Including endocrinopathies and other immune-related adverse events

late_effects.name: Chronic immunosuppression-related complications
late_effects.detail: In patients with ongoing immunosuppressive conditions

late_effects.name: Lymphedema surveillance
late_effects.detail: In patients who underwent lymph node dissection or regional radiation

late_effects.name: Dermatologic surveillance for NMSC
late_effects.detail: Given increased risk of second skin cancers
